hero.title:
National
Engineering
Science
Olympiad
hero.description: An innovative competition developing analytical thinking, teamwork, and an interdisciplinary approach — solving real engineering challenges in conditions that mirror actual scientific and industrial environments.

about.subtitle: About NEScO
about.title:
Science meets
real engineering
about.p1: Unlike traditional olympiads, NEScO offers participants the opportunity to solve real engineering challenges in conditions that closely resemble actual scientific and industrial environments. Teams work across disciplines — math, physics, chemistry, biology — to build genuine problem-solving competencies.
about.p2: In NEScO 2025, teams created and presented research on cancer drugs, antineutrino detection for nuclear power plants, and water filtration systems — all during the final round.

grant.title: Gold Medal Grant
grant.desc: 4 full bachelor's grants totaling $80,000+ from KBTU

prize.title: Prize Fund
prize.desc: $1,000 cash prize + $500 merch for tournament winners

expert.title: Expert Team
expert.desc: 10+ IPhO, IChO, IBO, EuPhO prize-winners + 5 professors

internship.title: Research Internships
internship.desc: Science internship opportunities with partner companies

stage1.title: First Stage
stage1.subtitle: Online · 2 Rounds
stage1.desc: Round 1 — 6–7 problems across math, physics, chemistry, and biology. 3 hours, captain submits. Round 2 — a case study received 2 days in advance, submitted as PDF or DOCX.

stage2.title: Second Stage
stage2.subtitle: In-person · KBTU, Almaty
stage2.desc: Round 1 — 8 interdisciplinary problems over 4–5 hours. Round 2 — case study with jury presentation. Appeals allowed within 5 hours of results.

cs.title: Case Study
cs.subtitle: Both Stages
cs.desc: Two cases: scientific and engineering. Written report up to 40 pages in APA 7 format. Originality ≥ 70%, AI content ≤ 20%. Plus a live presentation to the jury.

cta.subtitle: NEScO 2026
cta.title: Ready to compete?
cta.desc: Teams of 4–5 students from grades 9–12. Form your team, choose a captain, and register before the deadline.

rules.title:
Rules &
Regulations

reqItems.d1: 4 to 5 students per team, from grades 9 to 12.
reqItems.d2: Each team must select a captain responsible for registration, submissions, and communication with organizers.
reqItems.d3: All members may be from the same or different schools — provided this is agreed upon with the organizers in advance.
reqItems.d4: Phones, reference materials, and electronic devices are strictly prohibited unless explicitly allowed. Violations by any member may disqualify the entire team.

s1Items.d1: 6–7 interdisciplinary problems spanning mathematics, physics, chemistry, and biology. Teams have 3 hours to complete all problems. The captain is responsible for submitting the final solutions.
s1Items.d2: Teams receive their case 2 days before the submission deadline. The completed report is submitted electronically as a PDF or DOCX file.

s2Items.d1: 8 complex problems requiring cross-disciplinary reasoning. Teams have 4–5 hours. All members participate simultaneously.
s2Items.d2: Teams present their case study report to a panel of expert judges. Appeals against results are permitted within 5 hours of the results announcement.

csItems.d1: All written reports must follow APA 7th edition citation and formatting standards.
csItems.d2: The written report must not exceed 40 pages including references and appendices.
csItems.d3: Minimum originality score. AI-generated content must not exceed 20% of the total report.

rules.csNote2: one scientific case and one engineering case. Teams present their findings to a jury panel during the Second Stage final.

register.successDesc2: has been successfully registered for NEScO 2026. The captain will receive a confirmation email shortly.
register.subtitle: NEScO 2026
register.title: Register your team
register.desc: Open to teams of 4–5 students in grades 9–12. The team name must be unique. Participant 1 is the captain and primary contact.
